---
title: "douxing"
output: html_document
date: "2026-03-17"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.


```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(GGally)
#install.packages("FNN")
library(FNN)
library(pROC)
library(caret)
library(ROCR) 
library(class)      # For KNN implementation
library(corrplot)
library(MASS)
```


```{r}
data <- read.csv("student_productivity.csv")
data <- na.omit(data)
dim(data)
```


#logistic regression model
```{r}
#need to redefine
set.seed(43)
train_index <- sample(1:nrow(data), size = 0.8 * nrow(data))
df_train <- data[train_index, ]
df_test <- data[-train_index, ]

data$high_productivity <- ifelse(
  data$productivity_score >= median(data$productivity_score),
  1, 0
)
data$high_productivity <- factor(data$high_productivity, levels = c(0, 1))

table(data$high_productivity)  #do not need i think

# Simple logistic regression
log_model_simple <- glm(
  high_productivity ~ study_hours_per_day,
  data = df_train,
  family = binomial
)

summary(log_model_simple)

log_prob_simple <- predict(log_model_simple, newdata = df_test, type = "response")

log_pred_simple <- ifelse(log_prob_simple >= 0.5, 1, 0)
log_pred_simple <- factor(log_pred_simple, levels = c(0, 1))

confusionMatrix(log_pred_simple, df_test$high_productivity, positive = "1")

roc_simple <- roc(as.numeric(as.character(df_test$high_productivity)), log_prob_simple)
auc_simple <- auc(roc_simple)
auc_simple

plot(
  roc_simple,
  col = "blue",
  main = "ROC Curve for Simple Logistic Regression"
)

polygon(
  c(roc_simple$specificities, 0),
  c(roc_simple$sensitivities, 0),
  col = rgb(1, 0, 0, 0.3),
  border = NA
)

legend(
  "bottomright",
  legend = c("ROC", paste("AUC =", round(auc_simple, 3))),
  col = c("blue", rgb(1, 0, 0, 0.3)),
  lwd = c(1, NA),
  pch = c(NA, 15),
  pt.cex = 1,
  box.lty = 0,
  cex = 0.8
)

```

```{r}
head(data, 5)
```


#muti logistic regression model
```{r}
# stress_level(and phone_usage_hours) is high negative correlation with productivity_score
log_model_multiple <- glm(
  high_productivity ~ study_hours_per_day + focus_score + sleep_hours + stress_level + attendance_percentage,
  data = df_train,
  family = binomial
)
summary(log_model_multiple)

log_prob_multiple <- predict(log_model_multiple, newdata = df_test, type = "response")

log_pred_multiple <- ifelse(log_prob_multiple >= 0.5, 1, 0)
log_pred_multiple <- factor(log_pred_multiple, levels = c(0, 1))

confusionMatrix(log_pred_multiple, df_test$high_productivity, positive = "1")

roc_multiple <- roc(as.numeric(as.character(df_test$high_productivity)), log_prob_multiple)
auc_multiple <- auc(roc_multiple)
auc_multiple

plot(
  roc_multiple,
  col = "red",
  main = "ROC Curve for Multiple Logistic Regression"
)

polygon(
  c(roc_multiple$specificities, 0),
  c(roc_multiple$sensitivities, 0),
  col = rgb(1, 0, 0, 0.3),
  border = NA
)

legend(
  "bottomright",
  legend = c("ROC", paste("AUC =", round(auc_multiple, 3))),
  col = c("red", rgb(1, 0, 0, 0.3)),
  lwd = c(1, NA),
  pch = c(NA, 15),
  pt.cex = 1,
  box.lty = 0,
  cex = 0.8
)



#Predicted probabilities 
log_prob_simple <- predict(log_model_simple, newdata = df_test, type = "response")

log_pred_simple <- ifelse(log_prob_simple >= 0.5, 1, 0)
log_pred_simple <- factor(log_pred_simple, levels = c(0, 1))

confusionMatrix(log_pred_simple, df_test$high_productivity, positive = "1")
```
